Idiom (Old Frisian): Dij flechtiga foet is dijo jechtiga hand.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Balanced copular proverb: definite adjective + noun phrase equals definite adjective + noun phrase; uses synecdoche and alliteration.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person who flees from accusation is treated as if guilt has already been confessed.
Usage Contexts: Law-court speech; accusations; moral warning; narrative judgement; forensic inference.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Medieval Frisian legal proverb associated with Jurisprudentia Frisica; scholarship treats it as a compact legal maxim using foot and hand as body-part metonyms.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Old Frisian: /diː ˈflextiɣa foːt is diːjo ˈjextiɣa hand/
First Example Sentence: Tha thi mon fon there stede flach, seide thi asega: Dij flechtiga foet is dijo jechtiga hand.
Second Example Sentence: Ne scil thi skelta him swera leta, hwand dij flechtiga foet is dijo jechtiga hand.
Third Example Sentence: Tha liude nemen his ferde for jechte: dij flechtiga foet is dijo jechtiga hand.

Idiom (Old Frisian): Morth mot ma mith morthe kela.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Impersonal modal proverb: object-like noun + modal mot + generic pronoun ma + prepositional complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Grave wrongdoing demands an equally grave settlement or legally recognized retaliation.
Usage Contexts: Legal history; feud settlement; discussion of justice; literary severity; warning against escalation.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Old Frisian legal-proverb tradition; discussed under talion or retaliatory justice in medieval Frisian law scholarship.
First Example Sentence: In alder riuchte stod: morth mot ma mith morthe kela.
Second Example Sentence: Thi frana seide thet morth ma mith morthe kela scolde, sa tha magas frethe sochten.
Third Example Sentence: Tha boc lerde strangne dom: morth mot ma mith morthe kela.

Idiom (Old Frisian): Hwant nye syuckten byhowet nye ersedie.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Causal proverb introduced by hwant; plural noun subject + finite verb + plural object; parallel adjective repetition.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: New problems require new remedies rather than old routines.
Usage Contexts: Policy change; dispute resolution; innovation; medicine metaphor; practical advice.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Late medieval Old Frisian legal reasoning cites this formula to justify new treatment of unforeseen legal points.
First Example Sentence: Tha thi tol nie twivel brochte, seide thi red: Hwant nye syuckten byhowet nye ersedie.
Second Example Sentence: Ne halde ma alder sed al allena, hwand nye syuckten byhowet nye ersedie.
Third Example Sentence: Hwersa nie ned up riseth, tha byhowet nye syuckten nye ersedie.

Idiom (Old Frisian): Hwa tha punten naet bitallie mitta guede, dy schil bitallie mitta bloede.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Correlative conditional formula: hwa-clause followed by dy schil-clause; modal future with repeated verb and contrasting instruments.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone who refuses ordinary compensation will face harsher consequences.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Hwa net jeldath, tholath strangne bote.
Usage Contexts: Peace treaties; fines; contracts; public warnings; compensation law; negotiation.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Known from a fifteenth-century Westergo peace context; it compresses written settlement obligations into a memorable rhyming proverb.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Old Frisian: /hwa θa ˈpuntən naːt biˈtalliə ˈmita ˈɣweːdə dy skil biˈtalliə ˈmita ˈbloːdə/
First Example Sentence: Thi fredbref seide: hwa tha punten naet bitallie mitta guede, dy schil bitallie mitta bloede.
Second Example Sentence: Beter is gued to jeldane than blod to spillane, hwand hwa tha punten naet bitallie mitta guede, dy schil bitallie mitta bloede.
Third Example Sentence: Tha boden lezen tha kest: hwa tha punten naet bitallie mitta guede, dy schil bitallie mitta bloede.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Prepositional-infinitival idiom: mith + dative abstract noun + to-infinitive; state expression.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To live or stand under lawful peace, reconciliation, and social protection.
Usage Contexts: Reconciliation; family settlement; community order; court peace; everyday conflict resolution.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Attested Old Frisian legal phrase reflecting remnants of older case usage and the centrality of public peace in Frisian law.
First Example Sentence: Tha twa sibben wolden mith frethe to wasane.
Second Example Sentence: Thi asega bad tha burga mith frethe to wasane.
Third Example Sentence: Hwersa twa liude sone nimath, scelen hia mith frethe to wasane.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Elliptical prepositional legal formula: bi + official title + abstract noun; functions adverbially.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: According to authorized legal judgement rather than private anger or rumor.
Usage Contexts: Court procedure; arbitration; inheritance; civic administration; conflict mediation.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Asega means law-sayer; the phrase reflects Frisian oral-legal authority and the prestige of specialist judgement.
First Example Sentence: Thet scil gan bi asega wisdome, na bi mannes grame.
Second Example Sentence: Tha twa sibben deldon thiu erve bi asega wisdome.
Third Example Sentence: Thi red ne scil stande, hit ne ga bi asega wisdome.

Idiom (Old Frisian): Fresa ne thuruen nene herefert firer fara.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative necessity clause: ethnonym subject + ne thuruen + negative object + comparative adverb + infinitive.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A people or person should not be forced beyond the agreed limits of duty.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Tha Fresa nene firra herefert skelath.
Usage Contexts: Political rights; military obligation; autonomy; civic debate; boundary-setting.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Drawn from the Seventeen Statutes tradition, where Frisian military service is bounded geographically as part of Frisian freedom.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Old Frisian: /ˈfreːzɑ ne ˈθurwən ˈneːne ˈheːrəfert ˈfirər ˈfɑrɑ/
First Example Sentence: Thi kest seide: Fresa ne thuruen nene herefert firer fara.
Second Example Sentence: Tha liude weron fri, hwand Fresa ne thuruen nene herefert firer fara.
Third Example Sentence: Thi skelta ne mach hia firer lida: Fresa ne thuruen nene herefert firer fara.

Idiom (Old Frisian): Witha wilda heue and withene hethena here.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Coordinated alliterative prepositional phrases; two hostile nouns joined to intensify danger.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To face danger from both nature and human enemies at once.
Usage Contexts: Epic narration; border defense; sea culture; hardship; poetic description.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Formulaic language from Frisian freedom narrative material; combines North Sea landscape imagery with hostile-war imagery.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Old Frisian: /ˈwiθɑ ˈwildɑ ˈheːvə and ˈwiθene ˈheθenɑ ˈheːrə/
First Example Sentence: Tha stodon tha Fresa witha wilda heue and withene hethena here.
Second Example Sentence: Hwersa ned fon betha sidum cymth, is hit witha wilda heue and withene hethena here.
Third Example Sentence: Thi alde sang sprac fon there burg witha wilda heue and withene hethena here.

Idiom (Old Frisian): Wr hald ende wr haud di pannyng.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Spatial-metonymic legal formula: preposition wr + coordinated body-part nouns + definite monetary noun.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A payment or legal valuation touches the whole life of the person concerned.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Thi pannyng anlieth lif and bote.
Usage Contexts: Fines; capital redemption; monetary law; ritualized legal memory; institutional origin stories.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Associated with narratives about early Frisian legal and monetary privilege; neck and head stand for life and full personal liability.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Old Frisian: /wr̩ hɑld ˈende wr̩ hɑud di ˈpɑnnyŋɡ/
First Example Sentence: Tha thi mon grete bote achte, geng wr hald ende wr haud di pannyng.
Second Example Sentence: Thi skelta bad thone nia pannyng, hwand wr hald ende wr haud di pannyng.
Third Example Sentence: Bi des pawes hengnese stod wr hald ende wr haud di pannyng.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Reduplicative eternity formula: paired temporal adverbs + intensifying adverb + infinitive.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To remain valid permanently and beyond ordinary time limits.
Usage Contexts: Oaths; charters; donations; promises; legal continuity; solemn remembrance.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Formulaic phrasing in Old Frisian narrative and charter-like style; repetition strengthens memorability and permanence.
First Example Sentence: Tha Fresa wolden thet fridom emmer and emmermar ewelika stan.
Second Example Sentence: Thi bref segelde thiu iefte, thet hio emmer and emmermar ewelika stan.
Third Example Sentence: Ne breke ma thone freth, ther emmer and emmermar ewelika stan scil.
